does: This paper investigates test-time personalization for large language models by sampling multiple candidate outputs from a personalized policy and selecting among them using a personalized reward model. The authors establish that oracle selection achieves logarithmic utility growth with the number of samples, providing a theoretical upper bound. They identify why standard reward models fail to achieve this potential by deriving a unified scaling law that decomposes Best-of-N performance into four measurable components, uncovering two critical failure modes: user-level collapse where reward predictions become nearly constant for individual users, and query-level reward hacking where rewards anti-correlate with actual quality. To address these issues, they propose a probabilistic personalized reward model that learns user and query-specific variance terms to mitigate both failure modes.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of LLM personalization, which has traditionally focused on adapting model parameters or input prompts to individual users. It extends beyond single-pass inference paradigms by connecting to broader test-time scaling methods like best-of-N sampling, which have shown promise in non-personalized settings. The paper engages with reward model literature and scaling laws in deep learning, adapting theoretical frameworks to the personalized setting. It also relates to prior work on reward model reliability and alignment, particularly concerning how reward models can fail in systematic ways. The probabilistic approach draws on uncertainty quantification methods in machine learning, applying variance learning techniques to the personalization domain.
enables: This framework opens several research directions for improving LLM personalization at test time. The diagnostic decomposition provides a principled way to identify and measure reward model failures in other personalized AI systems beyond language models. The proposed variance-aware reward model suggests paths toward more robust personalized scoring mechanisms that could generalize across different user populations and query types. The theoretical connection between sampling and utility growth could motivate investigation of optimal sampling strategies tailored to individual users or query categories. This work also enables practical deployment strategies where inference-time computation scaling becomes a viable personalization mechanism without requiring expensive model retraining, potentially benefiting real-world personalization systems with heterogeneous user preferences and behavior patterns.
